workspace.renderError: The diagram could not be rendered. Check the syntax and try again.
workspace.fallbackBody: This workspace is wired into the site structure. A dedicated renderer will be added in the next build stage.
workspace.treeHint: Objects and arrays are expanded into a readable diagram tree.

footer.summary: Fast online diagram preview and conversion tools for developer documentation, architecture notes, product specs, and AI workflows.

metadata.title: Diagram Preview Tools - Mermaid, PlantUML, Markdown, Graphviz Online
metadata.description: Preview and convert Mermaid, PlantUML, Markdown, Graphviz, D2, draw.io, mind maps, JSON, YAML and developer diagrams online.

hero.subtitle: Preview Mermaid, PlantUML, Markdown, Graphviz, D2, draw.io, mind maps and developer diagrams online.

demo.title: See DiagramPreview turn generated text into visible diagrams
demo.description: Watch how AI-generated Mermaid, PlantUML, draw.io, Grafana, Prometheus, JSON, YAML, and developer source text can be previewed, debugged, converted, and exported from one browser workspace.
demo.videoFallback: Your browser does not support the video tag.

why: Use one focused workspace for text diagrams, documentation previews, and file-based diagram formats.
Most rendering runs in the browser, keeping drafts fast while reducing unnecessary round trips.
Every tool has its own URL, metadata, related tools, and export actions so it can grow as a search-friendly utility.

faq.question: What can DiagramPreview render?
faq.answer: The first release focuses on Mermaid, PlantUML, Markdown, Graphviz, JSON, YAML, and structured preview pages for the remaining diagram formats.

faq.question: Do diagrams upload to a server?
faq.answer: Browser-first tools render locally when possible. PlantUML uses a server URL in the first version so the renderer can be replaced later.

faq.question: Can I export diagrams?
faq.answer: Supported renderers expose SVG, PNG, copy, and print-style PDF actions from the workspace toolbar.

mermaid-preview.name: Mermaid Preview
mermaid-preview.shortDescription: Live Mermaid editor with SVG preview and export actions.
mermaid-preview.seoTitle: Mermaid Preview Online - Mermaid Editor and Live SVG Viewer
mermaid-preview.seoDescription: Preview Mermaid diagrams online with a live editor, readable examples, syntax review tips, SVG/PNG export, and copy actions for documentation.
mermaid-preview.intro: Write Mermaid syntax and preview flowcharts, sequence diagrams, classes, states, Gantt charts, and ER diagrams in a focused browser workspace.
mermaid-preview.emptyPreview: Your Mermaid diagram will appear here.

flowchart.label: Flowchart
flowchart.code:
flowchart TD
  A[Start] --> B{Ready?}
  B -- Yes --> C[Preview diagram]
  B -- No --> D[Edit source]
  D --> B
  C --> E[Export]

sequence.label: Sequence
sequence.code:
sequenceDiagram
  participant User
  participant App
  participant API
  User->>App: Paste diagram
  App->>API: Validate request
  API-->>App: OK
  App-->>User: Render preview

class.label: Class
class.code:
classDiagram
  class Diagram {
    +string source
    +render()
    +exportSvg()
  }
  class MermaidDiagram
  Diagram <|-- MermaidDiagram

state.label: State
state.code:
stateDiagram-v2
  [*] --> Editing
  Editing --> Rendering
  Rendering --> Ready
  Rendering --> Error
  Error --> Editing
  Ready --> Exported

gantt.label: Gantt
gantt.code:
gantt
  title Release plan
  dateFormat  YYYY-MM-DD

  Scaffold app :done, a1, 2026-06-01, 2d
  Tool preview :active, a2, after a1, 4d
  SEO pages :a3, after a2, 2d

er.label: ER diagram

howToUse: Paste Mermaid source, or start from a flowchart, sequence, class, state, Gantt, or ER sample.
Check the rendered SVG for broken syntax, cramped labels, reversed arrows, or branches that should be split into subgraphs.
Edit the source and wait for the preview to refresh before exporting SVG, PNG, or copying the source into a README.
When a generated diagram is too large, reduce it into smaller Mermaid diagrams rather than publishing a single unreadable graph.

faq.question: Why does a Mermaid diagram render in ChatGPT but fail here?
faq.answer: Large language models often omit required diagram headers, quote labels inconsistently, or mix syntax from different Mermaid versions. Start by checking the first line, node labels with punctuation, and unmatched brackets.

faq.question: What should I do when the diagram is visually too wide?
faq.answer: Split the source into smaller diagrams, use subgraphs for related nodes, shorten labels, and avoid turning a full system map into one giant flowchart.

faq.question: Can I export the preview for documentation?
faq.answer: Yes. When the SVG preview looks correct, export SVG for crisp documentation or PNG for quick sharing in issues and chat tools.

faq.question: How should I review AI-generated Mermaid before publishing it?
faq.answer: Check the diagram type, edge direction, invented nodes, long labels, and any labels that contain punctuation. Then keep the Mermaid source in the document so future edits remain reviewable.

seoBody: Mermaid Preview is a live editor for flowcharts, sequence diagrams, class diagrams, state diagrams, Gantt charts, and ER diagrams. It is useful when an AI assistant, README draft, or architecture note gives you Mermaid code but no reliable visual check.
Use the preview loop to catch syntax errors, unreadable labels, overly dense branches, and direction mistakes before the diagram lands in a pull request or documentation page.
The source remains editable, so you can keep the Mermaid text as the source of truth while exporting SVG or PNG only when you need a shareable artifact.

deepDives.title: Demo: turn a rough AI flowchart into readable Mermaid
deepDives.body: A common workflow is to ask an AI model for an architecture or incident flow, paste the Mermaid code here, then trim labels and branches until the preview is readable. The goal is not just valid syntax; the diagram should survive code review.
bullets: Keep the first line explicit, such as flowchart LR or sequenceDiagram.
Quote labels that contain punctuation, slashes, or parentheses.
Prefer a few named subgraphs over dozens of loose nodes.
deepDives.code:
flowchart LR
  user["User"] --> web["Web app"]
  web --> api["API gateway"]
  api --> auth["Auth service"]
  api --> orders["Order service"]
  orders --> db[(PostgreSQL)]
  orders --> queue["Event queue"]
deepDives.caption: This style keeps the graph compact while still showing the request path and async boundary.

deepDives.title: Mermaid preview checklist before publishing
deepDives.body: Before exporting, review the diagram like a reader who has not seen the source code. A technically valid Mermaid file can still be a poor documentation asset if labels are too long or relationships are ambiguous.
bullets: Every edge should answer what moves between two nodes.
Long product or API names should be shortened in the diagram and explained nearby.
If the diagram needs a paragraph to explain each arrow, split it into multiple diagrams.

deepDives.title: Common Mermaid errors this preview helps catch
deepDives.body: Most broken Mermaid snippets fail for a small set of reasons: missing diagram headers, unescaped labels, mixed diagram syntax, or relationships copied from prose without a valid node id.
bullets: If parsing fails on the first line, confirm the diagram starts with flowchart, sequenceDiagram, classDiagram, stateDiagram-v2, gantt, or erDiagram.
If labels contain slashes, colons, parentheses, or quotes, wrap the visible label in brackets and quotes.
If the graph renders but is unreadable, reduce scope before styling it.
deepDives.code:
flowchart LR
  api["API /checkout"] --> payment["Payment provider"]
  api --> queue["order.created event"]
deepDives.caption: Quoted labels make generated Mermaid more resilient when API paths or event names include punctuation.

plantuml-preview.name: PlantUML Preview
plantuml-preview.shortDescription: Preview PlantUML diagrams from text using a replaceable server renderer.
plantuml-preview.seoTitle: PlantUML Preview Online - PlantUML Editor and Viewer
plantuml-preview.seoDescription: Preview PlantUML sequence, class, use case, and activity diagrams online, then review server-rendered SVG output before publishing docs.
plantuml-preview.intro: Paste PlantUML text and generate a shareable SVG preview through the PlantUML server URL format.
plantuml-preview.emptyPreview: Your PlantUML preview will appear here.

sequence.label: Sequence
sequence.code:
@startuml
actor User
participant App
participant Service
User -> App: Submit diagram
App -> Service: Render
Service --> App: SVG
App --> User: Preview
@enduml

class.label: Class

usecase.label: Use case

activity.label: Activity

howToUse: Paste a complete PlantUML document that includes @startuml and @enduml.
Choose a sequence, class, use case, or activity sample when you need a known-good starting point.
Review the rendered SVG for missing participants, unreadable labels, styling that hides meaning, and remote include dependencies.
Reduce failing diagrams to a minimal source before adding skinparam, notes, sprites, or complex blocks back.
Export the rendered result only after confirming the source can still live beside the documentation.

faq.question: How does PlantUML rendering work?
faq.answer: The first release encodes the PlantUML source and displays the SVG generated by the PlantUML server.

faq.question: Can the renderer be replaced later?
faq.answer: Yes. The PlantUML URL builder is isolated behind a renderer module.

faq.question: What should I check before publishing a PlantUML diagram?
faq.answer: Confirm the source is complete, labels are short enough, remote includes are intentional, and the rendered SVG still matches the system or workflow being documented.

faq.question: Is server-rendered PlantUML suitable for private diagrams?
faq.answer: Use a trusted private renderer for sensitive diagrams. Server rendering can send PlantUML source to the renderer that creates the SVG.

seoBody: PlantUML Preview helps teams review UML-style diagrams without leaving the browser. It is useful for sequence flows, classes, activities, and use case diagrams that belong beside engineering documents.
The first version uses the standard PlantUML server URL approach. The encoding logic is isolated so a later local or private renderer can replace it without changing the page contract.
Because the editor uses plain text, diagrams can move cleanly between code review, wikis, issue trackers, and exported SVG assets.

deepDives.title: Demo: document a login sequence with PlantUML
deepDives.body: PlantUML is strong when a diagram needs to live beside code. A compact sequence source can explain authentication, token exchange, and failure handling without drawing by hand.
bullets: Name participants exactly as they appear in the system boundary.
Keep messages short enough for review comments and wiki pages.
Use alt blocks for expected failure paths instead of writing them only in prose.
deepDives.code:
@startuml
actor User
participant Web
participant Auth
database SessionDB
User -> Web: submit credentials
Web -> Auth: verify password
Auth -> SessionDB: create session
Auth --> Web: token
Web --> User: signed in
@enduml

deepDives.title: Troubleshooting: isolate server-rendered PlantUML failures
deepDives.body: When PlantUML rendering fails, the fastest path is to reduce the source until the server accepts it, then add styling, notes, and complex blocks back gradually.
bullets: First test @startuml and @enduml with two nodes only.
Check whether remote includes, sprites, or skinparam blocks depend on unavailable resources.
Keep private diagrams on trusted renderers because server rendering can send source to that renderer.

deepDives.title: Review pattern: keep PlantUML source maintainable
deepDives.body: PlantUML diagrams work best when the text source remains readable in code review. Heavy styling, large includes, and dense diagrams can make the rendered image look polished while the source becomes hard to maintain.
bullets: Keep participant and class names close to the terminology used in code or docs.
Use notes for assumptions instead of hiding them in long message labels.
Split unrelated class, sequence, and activity views into separate diagrams.
deepDives.code:
@startuml
participant Client
participant API
note over API: Validate token before reading orders
Client -> API: GET /orders
API --> Client: 200 OK
@enduml

markdown-preview.intro: Preview technical Markdown and render Mermaid fenced code blocks directly inside the document output.

howToUse: Paste Markdown into the editor.
Add Mermaid fenced blocks when a diagram belongs in the document.
Copy HTML or export the rendered result.

faq.question: Can Mermaid be rendered inside Markdown?
faq.answer: Yes. Fenced code blocks marked as mermaid are converted into SVG diagrams.

faq.question: Can I copy the generated HTML?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the copy action from the toolbar after the preview renders.

seoBody: Markdown Preview is designed for technical content where prose and diagrams live together. It helps writers check headings, lists, code blocks, and Mermaid diagrams before publishing to a repository or documentation site.
The preview supports GitHub-flavored Markdown and renders Mermaid fences as SVG, making it practical for README files, architecture decision records, and product specifications.
Use the copy and export actions when you need to move rendered content into another writing or review workflow.

deepDives.title: Demo: review Markdown docs with Mermaid blocks
deepDives.body: A Markdown preview becomes more useful when diagrams render beside headings, lists, and release notes. This catches broken fences before the document is pushed to GitHub, GitLab, or an internal wiki.
bullets: Use fenced code blocks with a language tag such as mermaid.
Keep diagram source close to the paragraph that explains it.
Preview tables and code fences together because one broken fence can corrupt the rest of the page.
deepDives.code:
# Checkout Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  Browser->>API: POST /checkout
  API->>Payment: authorize
  Payment-->>API: approved
```

deepDives.title: Publishing checklist: avoid Markdown preview surprises
deepDives.body: Markdown engines differ. A browser preview should help you catch structural problems, but final rendering still depends on the platform where the document will live.
bullets: Check heading order and avoid jumping from h1 to h4.
Verify code fences close before the next section starts.
Confirm that image URLs and Mermaid syntax are accepted by the target platform.
deepDives.code:
## Incident Summary

- Impact: checkout latency
- Fix: cache inventory lookup
- Follow-up: add p95 alert

graphviz-preview.name: Graphviz DOT Preview
graphviz-preview.shortDescription: Render DOT graphs into SVG directly in the browser.
graphviz-preview.seoTitle: Graphviz DOT Preview Online - DOT Viewer and SVG Renderer
graphviz-preview.seoDescription: Preview Graphviz DOT diagrams online with browser SVG rendering, examples, and export actions.
graphviz-preview.intro: Write DOT syntax and preview dependency graphs, state graphs, and directed diagrams as SVG.
graphviz-preview.emptyPreview: Your Graphviz diagram will appear here.

digraph.label: Digraph

dependency.label: Dependency graph

state.label: State graph

howToUse: Paste DOT syntax into the editor.
Use samples for directed graphs or dependency maps.
Export the SVG when the graph is ready.

faq.question: Does Graphviz run in the browser?
faq.answer: Yes. This page dynamically loads a WebAssembly-backed Viz renderer.

faq.question: Can I export the graph?
faq.answer: Rendered SVG can be downloaded or converted to PNG from the toolbar.

seoBody: Graphviz DOT Preview gives developers a quick way to turn DOT source into visual graphs. It is especially useful for dependency structures, directed workflows, state machines, and generated graph output.
The renderer loads only on the Graphviz page and returns SVG in the browser. That keeps the home page lightweight while still supporting real DOT rendering when the tool is opened.
Use it when inspecting build graphs, package relationships, state transitions, and architecture sketches that are easier to maintain as text.

deepDives.title: Demo: map service dependencies with DOT
deepDives.body: Graphviz DOT is useful for dependency maps where direction and clustering matter. A quick preview makes it easier to spot cycles, missing labels, and edges that should not cross a trust boundary.
bullets: Use subgraph cluster blocks for teams, networks, or bounded contexts.
Label edges when the relationship is not obvious from node names.
Prefer directed graphs for deployment and dependency diagrams.

deepDives.title: Debugging: reduce DOT layout noise
deepDives.body: DOT can render valid source into a confusing layout when ranks, clusters, and edge density compete. Debug the graph by removing styling first and restoring layout hints only after the relationships are clear.
bullets: Start with rankdir and plain node names.
Add clusters after the edge direction is correct.
Use fewer global style attributes when reviewing content correctness.

d2-preview.name: D2 Preview
d2-preview.shortDescription: Preview D2 architecture diagrams and service maps in a focused workspace.
d2-preview.seoTitle: D2 Preview Online - Render D2 Architecture Diagrams
d2-preview.seoDescription: Preview D2 diagram text for architecture sketches, service maps, dependency diagrams, and text-first developer documentation workflows.
d2-preview.intro: Draft D2 diagram text in the shared DiagramPreview workspace while the dedicated renderer is prepared.
d2-preview.emptyPreview: The D2 preview renderer is coming online.

architecture.label: Architecture
architecture.code:
client -> gateway -> api
api -> database
api -> queue

service.label: Service map
service.code:
web: Web app
api: API service
db: Database
web -> api -> db

sequence.label: Sequence
sequence.code:
user -> app: request
app -> api: fetch
api -> app: response

howToUse: Paste D2 text into the editor and start with relationships before styling.
Use samples to structure service maps, deployment zones, or architecture notes.
Keep identifiers stable so future edits do not unexpectedly reshape the diagram.
Write edge labels as protocols, events, or verbs when the relationship is not obvious.
Return after the renderer module is enabled for SVG output.

faq.question: Is D2 rendering enabled?
faq.answer: The route and workspace are ready. The dedicated renderer is planned for the next build stage.

faq.question: What makes a D2 diagram easier to review?
faq.answer: Stable node ids, short edge labels, nested containers for boundaries, and comments for assumptions make D2 diagrams easier to maintain as text.

faq.question: When should I use D2 instead of Mermaid?
faq.answer: Use D2 when you want text-first architecture diagrams with explicit containers, labels, and layout-friendly service relationships.

seoBody: D2 Preview is prepared as part of the DiagramPreview tool family so D2 diagrams can share the same editor, metadata, export patterns, and related tool navigation.
The first build wires the page into the site structure and sample system. A dedicated renderer can be added behind the same workspace without changing the public URL.
This page is intended for architecture sketches, service maps, and text-first system diagrams.

deepDives.title: Demo: draft a D2 service map
deepDives.body: D2 is readable for architecture diagrams that need labels, containers, and typed relationships. Even before final styling, a preview helps verify that the text describes the intended system.
bullets: Use nested blocks for domains or deployment zones.
Write relationship labels as verbs or protocols.
Keep identifiers stable so future edits do not reshuffle the whole diagram.
deepDives.code:
client -> gateway: HTTPS
gateway -> orders: REST
orders -> queue: publish event
queue -> worker: consume
orders -> db: SQL

deepDives.title: Review note: separate structure from visual polish
deepDives.body: The best D2 workflow starts with structure. Once nodes and edges are correct, apply themes and layout settings; otherwise styling can hide that the diagram is missing an important dependency.
bullets: Validate the graph in plain form first.
Only add icons or custom styles after the flow is correct.
Use comments to explain assumptions that are not visible in the graph.
deepDives.code:
# Assumption: analytics is async
api -> event_bus: order.created
event_bus -> analytics: consume

deepDives.title: Architecture note: model boundaries before dependencies
deepDives.body: D2 is useful for showing bounded contexts, deployment zones, and ownership boundaries. Start by modeling containers and then add dependencies inside or across those boundaries.
bullets: Use nested blocks for teams, networks, or runtime zones.
Name external dependencies explicitly so they are not mistaken for internal services.
Keep async and sync relationships visually distinguishable through labels.

drawio-preview.name: Draw.io Viewer
drawio-preview.shortDescription: Open draw.io and diagrams.net XML files online before sharing or converting them.
drawio-preview.seoTitle: Open draw.io File Online - diagrams.net Viewer and XML Preview
drawio-preview.seoDescription: Open draw.io and diagrams.net XML online to inspect pages, objects, labels, embedded data, and portability issues before review or SVG export.
drawio-preview.intro: Paste draw.io or diagrams.net XML to inspect pages, object counts, labels, and file structure before sharing, converting, or exporting the diagram.
drawio-preview.emptyPreview: A draw.io file summary will appear here.

xml.label: Minimal XML
xml.code: <mxfile host="DiagramPreview"><diagram name="System Context"><mxGraphModel><root><mxCell id="0"/><mxCell id="1" parent="0"/><mxCell id="app" value="Web app" vertex="1" parent="1"><mxGeometry x="120" y="80" width="120" height="60" as="geometry"/></mxCell></root></mxGraphModel></diagram></mxfile>

howToUse: Paste .drawio, diagrams.net, or mxfile XML into the editor.
Review page names, object counts, labels, and whether the XML is complete enough to share.
Check for default page names, hidden draft content, off-canvas objects, or external assets that may break in another workspace.
Use related converters when the diagram needs to move between editable draw.io XML and publishable SVG.
Keep the original XML in version control when the diagram is part of architecture or incident documentation.

faq.question: Does this render complex draw.io files?
faq.answer: The current workflow focuses on XML structure inspection, page metadata, and review signals. Use it to decide whether a file is complete and portable before editing or exporting.

faq.question: Can I open a draw.io file without installing diagrams.net?
faq.answer: Yes. Paste the .drawio XML content into the browser workspace to inspect pages, objects, and file structure before deciding whether to edit or export it.

faq.question: What should I check before sharing a draw.io file?
faq.answer: Check page names, hidden draft content, oversized labels, embedded images, and whether the XML loads cleanly before exporting SVG or PNG.

faq.question: When should I keep draw.io XML instead of only exporting SVG?
faq.answer: Keep the XML when teammates need to edit the diagram later. Export SVG for documentation, READMEs, tickets, or static publishing where editability is less important.

seoBody: Draw.io Viewer helps developers open draw.io and diagrams.net XML files online when a diagram needs a quick inspection before it goes into a README, wiki, issue, or handoff note.
Use it to check page names, XML structure, object counts, hidden draft content, and portability issues before converting or sharing the diagram.
The page also connects draw.io preview workflows with PlantUML to draw.io conversion, AI draw.io generation, and SVG export tools.

deepDives.title: Demo: inspect diagrams.net XML before sharing
deepDives.body: A draw.io file is XML underneath. Previewing it helps catch broken downloads, missing pages, oversized labels, and imported diagrams that still contain private draft nodes.
bullets: Check page names before sending the file to a reviewer.
Look for off-canvas objects and hidden scratch content.
Use SVG export only after the XML preview loads cleanly.
deepDives.code: <mxfile><diagram name="System"><mxGraphModel><root><mxCell id="0"/><mxCell id="1" parent="0"/></root></mxGraphModel></diagram></mxfile>

deepDives.title: Debug checklist: make draw.io files portable
deepDives.body: draw.io diagrams often move between Confluence, diagrams.net, Git repos, and exported SVGs. A portable file should keep labels, page boundaries, and embedded data intact.
bullets: Avoid relying on externally hosted images when the diagram must work offline.
Keep each page focused on one diagram or review topic.
Rename default pages so exports are easier to understand.
deepDives.code:
Page 1: System Context
Page 2: Checkout Sequence
Page 3: Deployment Notes

deepDives.title: Review pattern: separate edit source from publish artifact
deepDives.body: Treat draw.io XML as the editable source and SVG as the publishing artifact. That distinction keeps diagrams maintainable while still giving readers a clean static image.
bullets: Store the .drawio XML next to the documentation that references it.
Export SVG only after the page name, viewport, and labels are stable.
Mention the source file path near important architecture diagrams so future updates do not happen in a detached export.
deepDives.code:
docs/architecture/checkout.drawio
README.md -> embeds checkout.svg
PR review -> updates checkout.drawio first

seoBody: Mind Map Preview helps convert structured text into visual planning diagrams. It is aimed at product planning, research synthesis, and AI-assisted brainstorming workflows.
The route is present in the first build so it can share navigation, metadata, and search discovery with the rest of the tool site.
A dedicated mind map renderer can later plug into the same editor and export toolbar.

deepDives.title: Demo: turn release planning notes into a mind map
deepDives.body: Mind maps work well for messy planning material because they reveal grouping problems quickly. If a branch becomes too wide, the document probably needs another level of structure.
bullets: Use top-level headings for major themes.
Move operational details below the feature or risk they belong to.
Keep leaf nodes short so the preview stays scannable.
deepDives.code:
# Release Plan
## Checkout
- Payment retry
- Inventory lock
## Observability
- Error dashboard
- Alert tuning

deepDives.title: Editing pattern: use the map to restructure notes
deepDives.body: The preview should not only display a mind map; it should help you edit the source. Move branches around in text, re-render, and watch whether the structure becomes easier to explain.
bullets: Split a branch when it contains both decisions and tasks.
Promote repeated leaf nodes into a shared parent topic.
Use the map before writing long docs so the outline is stable.
deepDives.code:
Product Launch
  Messaging
    Landing page
    Social post
  Engineering
    Feature flag
    Rollback plan

faq.question: Which syntax does this tool use?
faq.answer: It uses Mermaid sequenceDiagram syntax and the Mermaid browser renderer.

seoBody: Sequence Diagram Preview is a focused editor for message flows between users, apps, services, databases, and external systems.
Because it uses Mermaid sequenceDiagram syntax, the source can live naturally in Markdown files, code review comments, and architecture notes.
Use the page for API call flows, login journeys, payment systems, and AI-generated interaction diagrams.

deepDives.title: Demo: preview an API request sequence
deepDives.body: Sequence diagrams are best when they show responsibility, not every internal function call. A focused preview helps reviewers confirm who calls whom and what the important return values are.
bullets: Show external users or systems as explicit participants.
Use notes for assumptions rather than adding fake service calls.
Add alt blocks for failure paths that need operational handling.
deepDives.code:
sequenceDiagram
  participant Browser
  participant API
  participant DB
  Browser->>API: POST /orders
  API->>DB: insert order
  DB-->>API: order id
  API-->>Browser: 201 Created

deepDives.title: Debugging: keep Mermaid sequence syntax small
deepDives.body: When a sequence diagram breaks, simplify it to participants and one message. Then restore loops, alternatives, and notes after the renderer accepts the base flow.
bullets: Declare participants when labels contain spaces or punctuation.
Keep arrows consistent for request and response messages.
Avoid mixing flowchart syntax inside sequenceDiagram blocks.
deepDives.code:
sequenceDiagram
  participant Web App
  participant API
  Web App->>API: health check
  API-->>Web App: ok

json-to-diagram.name: JSON to Diagram
json-to-diagram.shortDescription: Visualize JSON as a readable object and array tree.
json-to-diagram.seoTitle: JSON to Diagram Online - JSON Tree Visualizer
json-to-diagram.seoDescription: Convert JSON into an online tree diagram with syntax validation and readable object structure.
json-to-diagram.intro: Paste JSON and inspect nested objects and arrays as a structured tree diagram.
json-to-diagram.emptyPreview: Your JSON tree will appear here.
howToUse: Paste valid JSON.
Read the generated tree from root to leaves.
Fix syntax errors shown in the preview pane.

faq.question: Does the JSON parser validate syntax?
faq.answer: Yes. Invalid JSON produces a clear preview error instead of a broken page.

seoBody: JSON to Diagram turns nested JSON into a readable tree so developers can inspect API responses, configuration files, generated data, and AI output more quickly.
The tool keeps the raw JSON beside the visual structure. That makes it easier to find deep keys, array shapes, and unexpected values without losing the original source.
Use it when reviewing API contracts, debugging payloads, or explaining data structures to teammates.

deepDives.title: Demo: inspect an API payload as a tree
deepDives.body: JSON trees are useful when an API response is too nested to read as raw text. A visual preview makes it easier to find arrays, optional fields, and places where naming is inconsistent.
bullets: Expand one branch at a time for large payloads.
Check whether numeric-looking IDs are strings or numbers.
Use the preview before writing JSONPath or jq filters.

deepDives.title: Review checklist: find schema drift in raw JSON
deepDives.body: A JSON preview is often the first sign that backend and frontend assumptions diverged. Look for renamed fields, nulls, arrays that can be empty, and mixed object shapes.
bullets: Compare example payloads from success and error responses.
Mark fields that appear only for one role, locale, or feature flag.
Do not infer required fields from a single sample payload.
deepDives.code:
success.data.items[0].price
error.error.code
user.profile.locale

faq.question: Does YAML share logic with JSON diagrams?
faq.answer: Yes. YAML is parsed first and then visualized through the same tree model.

seoBody: YAML to Diagram helps make configuration files easier to read by turning nested values into a structured tree.
It is useful for CI workflows, Kubernetes manifests, static site config, and documentation examples where indentation matters.
The tool shares tree visualization logic with JSON to Diagram so future improvements can benefit both data formats.

deepDives.title: Demo: preview workflow YAML as a tree
deepDives.body: YAML indentation makes structure easy to write and easy to misread. A tree preview helps catch whether jobs, steps, environment variables, and nested configuration ended up under the intended parent.
bullets: Check arrays under steps, containers, rules, or hosts carefully.
Use the preview after copy-pasting snippets from documentation.
Review multi-document YAML one document at a time when possible.
deepDives.code:
name: CI
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: npm test

deepDives.title: Debugging: indentation is the real schema
deepDives.body: Many YAML errors are not parse errors; they are valid YAML with the wrong hierarchy. The preview should make those silent structure mistakes obvious before a deployment or CI run.
bullets: Compare sibling keys visually when a value seems ignored.
Avoid tabs and mixed indentation.
For Kubernetes, confirm metadata, spec, and template levels before checking container fields.
deepDives.code:
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: api
          image: ghcr.io/acme/api:latest

ai-diagram-generator.emptyPreview: Describe the diagram you want, choose a Mermaid diagram type, and let AI draft editable Mermaid source with live preview.

howToUse: Describe the diagram audience, scope, required nodes, and any systems the model must not invent.
Choose a Mermaid diagram type before generating so the output matches the task.
Generate editable Mermaid source, then preview the diagram immediately.
Remove invented components, fix labels, and split oversized results before sharing.
Export SVG or PNG only after the source is readable enough for future documentation updates.

faq.question: Which AI provider is used?
faq.answer: The server calls DeepSeek directly and uses a local template fallback when DeepSeek is unavailable or not configured.

faq.question: Are API keys exposed to the browser?
faq.answer: No. API keys are read only on the server through environment variables.

faq.question: How do I prevent AI from inventing architecture components?
faq.answer: List required systems, explicitly say what should not appear, and ask for a compact Mermaid diagram based only on the provided context.

faq.question: What should I do after the AI returns a diagram?
faq.answer: Preview it, compare it against source material, remove inaccurate nodes, shorten labels, and keep the Mermaid source editable in the final document.

seoBody: AI Diagram Generator turns natural language prompts into editable Mermaid diagrams for architecture notes, incident reports, product flows, and technical documentation.
The best workflow is not prompt to final image. Generate source, preview it, edit the Mermaid, and check whether the diagram matches the actual system before exporting.
The server calls DeepSeek directly and uses a local template fallback when DeepSeek is unavailable or not configured, while API keys remain on the server side.

deepDives.title: Demo: ask AI for a diagram that can be reviewed
deepDives.body: AI diagram generation works best when the prompt includes audience, scope, nodes, and the kind of decision the diagram should support. Vague prompts tend to create attractive but unreviewable diagrams.
bullets: Name the system boundary and the people who will read the diagram.
List required services and data stores so the model does not invent them.
Ask for Mermaid output that can be previewed and edited.
deepDives.code: Create a Mermaid architecture diagram for a B2B billing service. Audience: backend review. Include web app, API gateway, billing service, Stripe, invoice database, queue, worker, email provider, and admin dashboard. Show sync calls and async events.

deepDives.title: Review loop: AI output is a draft, not documentation truth
deepDives.body: The useful workflow is generate, preview, correct, and only then publish. The preview step is where missing services, invented dependencies, and unreadable labels become visible.
bullets: Delete invented components before exporting.
Shorten labels that wrap badly in the preview.
Compare the diagram against source docs, code, or architecture notes before using it in a decision.
deepDives.code: Prompt -> Mermaid source -> Browser preview -> Edit labels -> Export SVG/PNG -> Attach to PR

deepDives.title: Prompt recipe: constrain the diagram before generation
deepDives.body: A strong diagram prompt gives the model less room to improvise. Define the audience, diagram type, required nodes, forbidden assumptions, and output format before asking for Mermaid source.
bullets: Start with the reader: backend review, product spec, onboarding, or incident timeline.
List required systems and external services explicitly.
Ask for short labels and no extra components beyond the prompt.
deepDives.code: Audience: backend onboarding. Diagram type: flowchart LR. Required nodes: Browser, API Gateway, Auth Service, Orders Service, PostgreSQL, Redis. Do not add payments or analytics. Output Mermaid only.

text-to-mermaid.name: Text to Mermaid
text-to-mermaid.shortDescription: Convert product notes, incidents, and architecture text into Mermaid diagrams.
text-to-mermaid.seoTitle: Text to Mermaid Online - Generate Mermaid Diagrams from Prompts
text-to-mermaid.seoDescription: Convert product notes, incidents, architecture text, and process descriptions into editable Mermaid diagrams with AI-assisted generation and preview.
text-to-mermaid.intro: Paste plain text requirements, notes, or process descriptions and convert them into Mermaid code you can preview and export.
text-to-mermaid.emptyPreview: Paste plain text requirements, notes, or process descriptions and convert them into Mermaid code you can preview and export.

requirements.label: Requirements
requirements.prompt: Users paste diagram code, the app validates syntax, renders a preview, allows export, and suggests related tools.

incident.label: Incident
incident.prompt: A customer reports a broken export. Support checks logs, engineering reproduces, a fix is deployed, and support confirms resolution.

architecture.label: Architecture
architecture.prompt: Browser sends source text to a Next.js API route. The API calls DeepSeek, then returns Mermaid code for browser preview.

howToUse: Describe the audience, scope, required actors, and the decision the diagram should support.
Choose the Mermaid diagram type before generating so the result matches the problem: flowchart, sequenceDiagram, classDiagram, or ER.
Generate Mermaid code, then preview it immediately instead of copying AI output directly into documentation.
Remove invented components, shorten labels, and split oversized results into smaller diagrams.
Export SVG or PNG only after the source remains readable enough for future edits.

faq.question: Which AI provider is used?
faq.answer: The server calls DeepSeek directly and uses a local template fallback when DeepSeek is unavailable or not configured.

faq.question: Are API keys exposed to the browser?
faq.answer: No. API keys are read only on the server through environment variables.

faq.question: Can I generate a Mermaid sequence diagram from plain text?
faq.answer: Yes. Describe the actors and request order, choose sequenceDiagram, then review the generated Mermaid code before exporting it.

faq.question: What should I include in a good text-to-Mermaid prompt?
faq.answer: Include the audience, diagram type, required actors or systems, important data stores, external services, and anything the model must not invent.

faq.question: Should generated Mermaid be treated as final documentation?
faq.answer: No. Treat it as a draft. Preview the output, verify it against source material, and keep the Mermaid code editable in the final document.

seoBody: Text to Mermaid turns plain-language requirements, incident notes, user journeys, and architecture descriptions into Mermaid diagrams for documentation.
It is useful when a developer has the flow in prose but needs editable Mermaid code for a README, design review, incident report, or product spec.
After generation, keep the Mermaid source editable, preview the SVG, and export only when labels, arrows, and diagram type match the story.

deepDives.title: Demo: convert incident notes into Mermaid
deepDives.body: Incident notes often contain actors, events, and timing in prose. Converting them into Mermaid can reveal missing steps, unclear ownership, and recovery actions that were buried in text.
bullets: Keep the input factual and chronological.
Ask for sequenceDiagram when order matters.
Ask for flowchart when the goal is decision or remediation logic.
deepDives.code: At 10:05 the API started returning 503. The gateway retried twice, then the checkout service timed out while waiting for inventory. The worker queue kept accepting events. Recovery was to scale inventory pods and drain retries.

deepDives.title: Editing pattern: choose the Mermaid diagram type first
deepDives.body: A text-to-diagram result is stronger when the diagram type matches the question. Architecture notes, incidents, and product flows should not all become the same flowchart.
bullets: Use sequenceDiagram for request order and ownership.
Use flowchart for decision trees and user journeys.
Use ER diagrams for data relationships instead of forcing them into boxes and arrows.
deepDives.code:
Question: who called whom? -> sequenceDiagram
Question: what path can the user take? -> flowchart
Question: what data relates? -> erDiagram

deepDives.title: Prompt template: generate a diagram reviewers can trust
deepDives.body: The strongest prompts constrain the model. Instead of asking for a beautiful diagram, ask for a Mermaid source file that names the systems, keeps labels short, and avoids unverified services.
bullets: State the diagram audience and scope in the first sentence.
List required nodes and any nodes that must not appear.
Ask the model to keep edge labels short enough for a README or architecture decision record.
deepDives.code: Create a Mermaid sequenceDiagram for backend reviewers. Scope: checkout payment authorization only. Required actors: Browser, Checkout API, Auth service, Payment provider, Order database. Do not add inventory or shipping. Keep message labels under 5 words.

mermaid-ai-fixer.seoDescription: Fix broken Mermaid syntax with AI, preserve diagram intent, preview the repaired source, and improve readability before exporting.

brokenFlow.prompt: Fix the syntax and keep the same flow.

howToUse: Paste the broken Mermaid code into the editor and describe the repair goal.
Ask for a minimal syntax fix first when the diagram structure is already correct.
Preview the repaired source before asking for readability improvements.
Compare the fixed diagram with the original intent so the AI does not silently redesign the flow.
Export only after the repaired Mermaid code remains understandable.

faq.question: Which AI provider is used?
faq.answer: The server calls DeepSeek directly and uses a local template fallback when DeepSeek is unavailable or not configured.

faq.question: Are API keys exposed to the browser?
faq.answer: No. API keys are read only on the server through environment variables.

faq.question: Should I ask for a redesign or a syntax fix?
faq.answer: Ask for a minimal syntax fix first. Once the diagram renders, you can separately ask for shorter labels, subgraphs, or layout improvements.

faq.question: Can AI change the meaning of a Mermaid diagram while fixing it?
faq.answer: Yes. Always compare the repaired diagram with the original source and check whether participants, edges, states, and branches still mean the same thing.

seoBody: Mermaid AI Fixer repairs broken Mermaid syntax and helps make generated diagrams readable without losing the original intent.
Use it when ChatGPT, another AI tool, or a copied README snippet returns Mermaid that fails to render because of labels, arrows, missing headers, or mixed syntax.
The safest workflow is minimal fix, preview, then optional readability cleanup. The server calls DeepSeek directly and uses a local template fallback when DeepSeek is unavailable.

deepDives.title: Demo: repair a broken Mermaid flowchart
deepDives.body: AI-generated Mermaid often fails because labels contain reserved punctuation, arrows are mixed, or subgraph syntax is incomplete. A fixer should return valid code and keep the original intent.
bullets: Preserve node meaning while simplifying labels.
Quote labels that include slashes, colons, or parentheses.
Render the fixed diagram immediately to confirm the repair.
deepDives.code:
flowchart LR
  A[User submits /checkout] --> B{Payment OK?}
  B -- yes --> C[Create order]
  B -- no --> D[Show retry]

deepDives.title: Debugging: fix syntax before improving style
deepDives.body: The repair loop should solve rendering first. Once the diagram renders, then improve grouping, labels, and layout; otherwise the model may hide the original syntax error behind a redesign.
bullets: Ask for the smallest valid patch when the structure is already good.
Ask for readability improvements only after render errors are gone.
Keep a copy of the broken input for regression testing.
deepDives.code: Broken -> minimal fixed Mermaid -> rendered preview -> optional refactor

deepDives.title: Repair checklist: preserve meaning while fixing syntax
deepDives.body: A Mermaid fixer should not turn an error into a different diagram. Keep the semantic pieces stable, then make the source valid and readable.
bullets: Check that every original participant, state, or node still exists unless you asked to remove it.
Verify edge direction and branch labels after the repair.
Keep a copy of the broken input so you can compare before and after.
deepDives.code:
Original intent: User submits checkout -> API validates -> Payment authorizes -> Order created
Repair goal: valid Mermaid only, no new services

openapi-to-sequence.name: OpenAPI to Sequence Diagram
openapi-to-sequence.shortDescription: Convert OpenAPI paths into a Mermaid sequence diagram locally.
openapi-to-sequence.seoTitle: OpenAPI to Sequence Diagram Online - API Flow Visualizer
openapi-to-sequence.seoDescription: Convert OpenAPI JSON or YAML paths into Mermaid sequence diagrams for API documentation, backend reviews, onboarding, and incident notes.
openapi-to-sequence.intro: Paste OpenAPI JSON or YAML and turn API operations into a sequence diagram that explains request handling.

howToUse: Paste OpenAPI JSON or YAML that includes the paths you want to explain.
Review the generated sequence diagram for actors, request methods, response branches, and missing operations.
Simplify large specs by keeping only the paths relevant to the document you are writing.
Export the diagram after checking that endpoint names and response labels are readable.

faq.question: Should I paste a full OpenAPI document?
faq.answer: You can, but focused path snippets usually produce clearer diagrams. Remove unrelated endpoints when writing a specific doc page.

faq.question: What does the sequence diagram represent?
faq.answer: It summarizes request and response relationships from the OpenAPI paths. It does not execute the API or prove runtime behavior.

faq.question: How do I make the output easier to read?
faq.answer: Group related endpoints, shorten operation summaries, and avoid mixing unrelated resources in one sequence diagram.

faq.question: Can this prove what happens in production?
faq.answer: No. It explains the contract in diagram form. Compare it with HAR files, Postman collections, logs, or traces when you need to verify runtime behavior.

faq.question: Which OpenAPI fields improve the sequence diagram?
faq.answer: operationId, summary, security schemes, requestBody, response descriptions, and focused path groups all make the generated messages easier to understand.

seoBody: OpenAPI to Sequence Diagram converts API paths, operations, parameters, and response shapes into a Mermaid sequence diagram for documentation and backend review.
It helps explain endpoint behavior when a specification is too large to read quickly or when AI-generated docs need a visual sanity check.
Use it for onboarding, API design reviews, incident notes, and release documentation where request flow matters more than a complete schema dump.

deepDives.title: Demo: document one checkout endpoint instead of the whole API
deepDives.body: A sequence diagram is most useful when it explains one workflow. For OpenAPI, that usually means selecting a small group of related paths instead of the entire specification.
bullets: Keep POST /checkout and the most relevant success and error responses.
Use operationId or summary fields to create readable message labels.
Link to the full OpenAPI spec from the document instead of copying everything into the diagram.
deepDives.code:
paths:
  /checkout:
    post:
      operationId: createCheckout
      summary: Create checkout session
      responses:
        "201": { description: Checkout created }
        "402": { description: Payment required }
deepDives.caption: This focused input creates a cleaner sequence diagram than a full API dump.

deepDives.title: API review: turn a spec endpoint into a request story
deepDives.body: OpenAPI describes endpoints, but reviewers often need to understand the request story: who calls the endpoint, which auth step happens first, which services are involved, and what error paths matter.
bullets: Start from the path, method, auth scheme, request body, and main response codes.
Add backend services as inferred participants only when the contract names them clearly.
Use the sequence output to discuss behavior, not as a replacement for the OpenAPI spec.
deepDives.code:
POST /orders
Authorization: Bearer <token>
201 Created -> orderId
409 Conflict -> duplicate cart or inventory lock

deepDives.title: Troubleshooting: compare contract diagrams with runtime evidence
deepDives.body: OpenAPI sequence diagrams are strongest when paired with runtime evidence. Use the contract diagram to show intended behavior, then compare HAR, Postman, or trace data when an incident suggests the real call order is different.
bullets: Use OpenAPI for expected request and response paths.
Use HAR or Postman collections for observed request order.
Use the mismatch as the discussion point in backend review or incident follow-up.
deepDives.code:
Expected: Browser -> API -> Payment provider -> API -> Browser
Observed: Browser -> API -> API retry -> Payment provider timeout -> 503

sql-to-er-diagram.name: SQL to ER Diagram
sql-to-er-diagram.shortDescription: Convert CREATE TABLE SQL into a Mermaid ER diagram.
sql-to-er-diagram.seoTitle: SQL to ER Diagram Online - Visualize Table Relationships
sql-to-er-diagram.seoDescription: Convert CREATE TABLE SQL into Mermaid ER diagrams to review tables, primary keys, foreign keys, join tables, and schema relationships.
sql-to-er-diagram.intro: Paste SQL DDL and visualize tables, columns, primary keys, and foreign key relationships as an ER diagram.
sql-to-er-diagram.emptyPreview: Your ER diagram will appear here.

commerce.label: Commerce

blog.label: Blog

billing.label: Billing

howToUse: Paste CREATE TABLE statements that include primary keys and foreign key relationships.
Review the generated ER diagram for missing tables, unexpected one-to-many relationships, and join tables.
Check *_id columns that imply relationships but do not have explicit constraints.
Trim unrelated tables when documenting one bounded context or feature area.
Export the diagram after the relationships match the schema you intend to publish.

faq.question: Does this use AI?
faq.answer: No. This tool runs parsing and visualization locally in the browser.

faq.question: What SQL should I paste for the clearest ER diagram?
faq.answer: Use focused CREATE TABLE statements with primary keys, foreign keys, and join tables. Avoid dumping unrelated schemas into one diagram.

faq.question: Can this find missing foreign key constraints?
faq.answer: It can make suspicious relationship patterns visible, such as *_id columns without constraints, but you should confirm the database rules in migrations or schema docs.

seoBody: SQL to ER Diagram converts CREATE TABLE DDL into Mermaid ER diagrams for database documentation, schema review, and onboarding.
Use it to inspect tables, columns, primary keys, foreign keys, join tables, and relationship direction before a migration or documentation update ships.
DiagramPreview keeps the SQL source editable so you can revise a focused schema snippet and regenerate the visualization quickly.

deepDives.title: Demo: convert CREATE TABLE statements into an ER diagram
deepDives.body: SQL schema review becomes faster when tables, primary keys, and foreign keys are visible. The preview helps teams discuss relationships before migrations reach production.
bullets: Include CREATE TABLE statements for all referenced tables.
Keep foreign key constraints in the sample when possible.
Use the ER output to review relationships, not query performance.
deepDives.code:
CREATE TABLE users (id INT PRIMARY KEY, email TEXT);
CREATE TABLE orders (id INT PRIMARY KEY, user_id INT REFERENCES users(id), total DECIMAL);

deepDives.title: Review checklist: catch schema relationship mistakes
deepDives.body: An ER preview can expose missing foreign keys, many-to-many join tables, and columns that imply a relationship but are not constrained.
bullets: Look for *_id columns without a matching relationship.
Check whether join tables need composite uniqueness.
Confirm nullable foreign keys represent a real optional relationship.
deepDives.code:
order_items(order_id, product_id)
UNIQUE(order_id, product_id)

deepDives.title: Migration review: diagram only the tables that changed
deepDives.body: For database migrations, a smaller ER diagram is usually more useful than a full schema dump. Focus on the tables touched by the migration and their nearest dependencies.
bullets: Include referenced parent tables so foreign keys have context.
Call out new nullable foreign keys because they often change application behavior.
Use the diagram in PR review alongside the migration diff.

json-schema-visualizer.name: JSON Schema Visualizer
json-schema-visualizer.shortDescription: Inspect JSON Schema properties, types, required fields, and composition groups.
json-schema-visualizer.seoTitle: JSON Schema Visualizer Online - API Contract Tree Viewer
json-schema-visualizer.seoDescription: Visualize JSON Schema properties, required fields, arrays, enums, and oneOf/anyOf branches for API contract review and documentation.
json-schema-visualizer.intro: Paste JSON Schema and inspect a structured tree of properties, types, required fields, arrays, enums, and composition groups.
json-schema-visualizer.emptyPreview: Your schema tree will appear here.
howToUse: Paste a JSON Schema object or load a sample payload contract.
Inspect required fields, optional fields, array item types, enums, and composition groups.
Check whether descriptions and examples are present before publishing API documentation.
Export or copy the visualization after confirming it matches the validator used by your application.

faq.question: What schema features are most important to review?
faq.answer: Required fields, nullable behavior, array item schemas, enum values, additionalProperties, and oneOf/anyOf composition usually cause the most integration confusion.

faq.question: Can it validate data against the schema?
faq.answer: This page focuses on visualization and review. Use your application validator or a dedicated JSON Schema validator for final validation.

faq.question: How do I make a schema visualization easier to read?
faq.answer: Split very large schemas by resource, keep descriptions short, and avoid deeply nested anonymous objects when a named schema would be clearer.

faq.question: Can this help with breaking-change review?
faq.answer: Yes. Use it to spot newly required fields, enum changes, nested array changes, nullable behavior, and oneOf/anyOf branches that may affect clients.

faq.question: Should I visualize generated schemas from AI or code tools?
faq.answer: Yes, but review the output carefully. Generated schemas can invent optional metadata, miss required fields, or simplify composition rules in ways that do not match your validator.

seoBody: JSON Schema Visualizer turns properties, required fields, arrays, enums, oneOf, anyOf, and nested object structures into a reviewable schema map.
It is useful when API payload contracts are too nested for a table, or when AI-generated schema needs a quick check before being committed.
Use it to review breaking changes, explain request bodies, compare schema sections, and prepare documentation examples that match validation rules.

deepDives.title: Demo: review a request schema before API docs publish
deepDives.body: Schema diagrams are most useful when they reveal which fields are required, which arrays contain objects, and where composition changes behavior.
bullets: Required fields should stand out from optional metadata.
Enums should be visible because they often become UI states or SDK constants.
oneOf and anyOf need human explanation in docs, not only a visual branch.
deepDives.caption: This small schema already shows required fields, enum states, and open metadata.

deepDives.title: Schema review: find breaking changes before clients do
deepDives.body: JSON Schema diagrams are especially useful during API evolution. They make required fields, enum changes, nested arrays, nullable fields, and oneOf branches easier to review before a schema update ships.
bullets: Treat newly required fields as breaking unless every client already sends them.
Review enum additions with downstream validation rules.
Flatten deeply nested oneOf or anyOf branches into review notes when the diagram becomes too dense.

deepDives.title: Documentation pattern: pair schema trees with examples
deepDives.body: A schema tree explains structure, but developers still need examples. Use the visualization to show required fields and branches, then place a small request or response example next to it.
bullets: Use the tree for structure and the example for realistic values.
Call out enum values that map to user-visible states.
Explain oneOf or anyOf branches in prose when the visual tree becomes dense.
deepDives.caption: Examples make a schema visualization more useful for SDK users and API consumers.

deepDives.title: Demo: generate a system architecture diagram from notes
deepDives.body: Architecture notes usually mix components, deployment details, and product behavior. A good generated diagram separates boundaries and shows only the dependencies that matter for review.
bullets: Include users, external providers, services, queues, and data stores.
State whether the diagram should show runtime flow or deployment topology.
Ask the model to keep labels short for preview and export.
deepDives.code: Generate a Mermaid architecture diagram for a multi-tenant SaaS. Include browser, CDN, Next.js app, API gateway, tenant service, billing service, Postgres, Redis, event bus, worker, and email provider. Highlight tenant isolation boundary.

deepDives.title: Architecture review: identify what the diagram should prove
deepDives.body: A useful architecture diagram answers a question: where data flows, what can fail, which boundary protects sensitive data, or how a service scales. Without that question, generated diagrams tend to become decorative.
bullets: Write the review question in the prompt.
Remove details that do not support that question.
Keep security and data ownership boundaries visible.
deepDives.code:
Review question: can checkout continue if analytics is down?
Diagram focus: synchronous checkout path vs async analytics path.

deepDives.title: Review checklist: keep generated PlantUML maintainable
deepDives.body: PlantUML can become hard to maintain when AI adds too much styling or invents abstractions. Keep the first version plain and focused, then add style once the logic is correct.
bullets: Prefer readable participant names over clever aliases.
Avoid large skinparam blocks in the first draft.
Commit the source text beside the document that uses the rendered image.

deepDives.title: Demo: inspect XML feeds and config trees
deepDives.body: XML becomes easier to review when elements, attributes, and nested text are shown as a tree. This helps with RSS feeds, SVG snippets, SOAP payloads, and vendor configuration files.
bullets: Check attributes separately from child elements.
Look for repeated elements that should become arrays in downstream code.
Use the preview before writing XPath selectors.

deepDives.title: Debugging: namespace and malformed XML issues
deepDives.body: A visual tree makes namespace problems more obvious, but malformed XML must be fixed first. Start by confirming a single root element and properly closed tags.
bullets: Check xmlns declarations near the root.
Escape ampersands and angle brackets inside text.
Validate small fragments before pasting a full integration payload.
deepDives.code: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><text x="10" y="20">OK</text></svg>

deepDives.body: CSV previews are useful when you need to inspect headers, column order, and sample values before loading data into a database, spreadsheet, or visualization.
bullets: Check whether the first row is really a header.
Look for commas inside quoted fields.

deepDives.title: Data QA checklist: catch lightweight CSV problems
deepDives.body: CSV is simple until delimiters, quoting, encodings, and partial exports get involved. A preview should reveal shape problems before downstream tools produce confusing errors.
bullets: Compare row lengths against the header count.
Inspect date and numeric fields as strings before conversion.
Check for duplicate IDs or unexpected blank rows.

howToUse: Paste docker-compose.yml into the editor, including services, networks, volumes, and depends_on blocks.
Review service dependencies separately from readiness or health-check guarantees.
Check which services expose ports, mount volumes, or share networks before documenting the stack.
Trim secret values and environment variables before sharing a diagram publicly.
Export the diagram for onboarding docs, README files, or local development setup notes.

faq.question: Does this tool use AI?
faq.answer: No. It parses and visualizes the source locally in the browser.

faq.question: Does depends_on mean a service is ready?
faq.answer: No. depends_on shows startup ordering, not application readiness. Use health checks and application-level retries for readiness guarantees.

faq.question: What should I remove before sharing a Compose diagram?
faq.answer: Remove secrets, private registry names, internal hostnames, and environment variables that should not appear in public documentation.

seoBody: Docker Compose Diagram turns docker-compose.yml into a readable service topology for local stack documentation and onboarding.
Use it to visualize services, depends_on relationships, exposed ports, networks, and volumes before sharing setup instructions or reviewing a generated Compose file.
The diagram is a review surface: it explains declared topology but does not prove runtime readiness, health, or connectivity.

deepDives.title: Demo: visualize a Compose web stack
deepDives.body: Docker Compose files describe a local architecture in YAML. A diagram makes service dependencies, exposed ports, networks, and volumes easier to review before onboarding or debugging.
bullets: Show depends_on separately from actual runtime readiness.
Mark public ports and internal-only services.
Group observability, app, and data services when the file grows.
deepDives.code:
services:
  web:
    build: .
    depends_on: [api]
    ports: ["3000:3000"]
  api:
    image: acme/api
    depends_on: [db]
  db:
    image: postgres:16

deepDives.title: Review checklist: Compose diagrams are not readiness guarantees
deepDives.body: The diagram helps explain topology, but Compose depends_on does not always mean a dependency is ready for traffic. Pair the visual review with healthchecks and environment checks.
bullets: Add healthcheck for databases and queues.
Check whether ports expose services unintentionally.
Review named volumes when data persistence matters.
deepDives.code:
healthcheck:
  test: ["CMD", "pg_isready", "-U", "postgres"]
  interval: 10s
  retries: 5

deepDives.title: Security note: sanitize Compose before publishing
deepDives.body: Compose files often include environment variables, host paths, private registry names, and internal service names. A diagram makes topology easier to share, but the source should still be reviewed first.
bullets: Remove secrets and tokens from environment blocks.
Replace private registry URLs or internal hostnames when publishing externally.
Keep volume paths generic when they expose local machine structure.
deepDives.code:
environment:
  DATABASE_URL: postgres://user:***@db:5432/app
volumes:
  - ./data:/var/lib/postgresql/data

  name: diagram-preview
  namespace: production

    app: diagram-preview

        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix

howToUse: Paste one or more Kubernetes YAML manifests into the editor.
Review resource kind, namespace, labels, selectors, container images, ports, and ingress routes.
Check that Service selectors match Pod template labels before applying changes.
Inspect risky fields such as image tags, env vars, resource limits, probes, and hostPath volumes.
Export the visualization for release notes, PR review, or onboarding docs.

faq.question: Does this tool use AI?
faq.answer: No. It parses and visualizes the source locally in the browser.

faq.question: Can this prove a Kubernetes manifest will deploy successfully?
faq.answer: No. It helps review structure and relationships. Use kubectl, policy checks, and cluster validation for final deployment confidence.

faq.question: What Kubernetes mistakes are easiest to spot visually?
faq.answer: Selector and label mismatches, wrong namespaces, surprising image tags, missing probes, exposed ports, and risky volume mounts are easier to catch in a resource tree.

seoBody: Kubernetes Manifest Visualizer turns YAML manifests into readable resource trees for Deployments, Services, Ingress, ConfigMaps, labels, selectors, and namespaces.
Use it before release review to inspect image tags, ports, probes, resource limits, selectors, and routing assumptions without reading a long manifest line by line.
The tool supports documentation and review workflows; final deployment validation should still happen with Kubernetes-native commands and policy checks.

deepDives.title: Demo: inspect a Deployment and Service together
deepDives.body: Kubernetes YAML is easier to review when resources are grouped by namespace, labels, selectors, and exposed ports. The preview helps catch mismatched labels before kubectl apply.
bullets: Compare Service selectors with Pod template labels.
Check image tags and pull policies during release review.
Inspect resource requests and limits next to replica counts.
deepDives.code:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: api
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels: {app: api}
  template:
    metadata:
      labels: {app: api}

deepDives.title: Release checklist: find risky manifest changes
deepDives.body: A manifest visualizer should surface production-sensitive fields first: ingress hosts, service type, image tags, probes, secrets, and resources.
bullets: Treat LoadBalancer and NodePort changes as exposure changes.
Verify liveness and readiness probes before increasing replicas.
Do not paste real secret values into public tools.
deepDives.code:
service.type: ClusterIP -> LoadBalancer
image.tag: 1.8.2 -> latest
resources.limits.cpu: 500m

deepDives.title: Debug pattern: selectors before containers
deepDives.body: When a Service has no endpoints, the container image is often not the first thing to inspect. Start by comparing Service selectors with Pod template labels.
bullets: Check metadata.labels and spec.selector side by side.
Confirm namespace consistency for related resources.
Review targetPort names when Services reference named container ports.
deepDives.code:
Service selector: app=checkout
Pod labels: app=checkout-api
Result: Service has no matching endpoints
